does: This paper introduces ASTRA, a defense mechanism that protects Vision Language Models from adversarial attacks by identifying and removing harmful feature directions. The core method constructs steering vectors by randomly ablating visual tokens from adversarial images and identifying those most strongly correlated with jailbreak attempts. At inference time, ASTRA applies adaptive activation steering to project out these harmful directions from the model's internal representations, effectively steering the model away from generating unsafe content. The approach is designed to be computationally efficient compared to existing defenses, operating at inference time without requiring expensive retraining or comprehensive input preprocessing, while maintaining the model's ability to generate appropriate responses to benign inputs.
connects: This work builds on recent research into adversarial robustness of vision-language models, which represents an emerging frontier as these multimodal systems become more powerful and deployed. It extends prior work on steering vectors and activation steering—techniques developed in mechanistic interpretability and control of large language models—to the multimodal setting where vision components introduce novel attack surfaces. The paper responds to limitations of existing defenses like adversarial training, which is computationally expensive, and response evaluation methods, which add significant latency. It leverages insights from work on identifying transferable adversarial features and token importance in vision transformers, combining these threads into a practical defense framework.
enables: This work opens directions for more efficient and scalable defenses against multimodal adversarial attacks. By demonstrating that harmful behavior can be localized to specific feature directions, it suggests that future defenses might systematically characterize and remove unsafe capabilities without full model retraining. The token ablation approach for identifying vulnerability sources could enable more targeted hardening of specific visual processing pathways. More broadly, this work points toward adaptive steering as a general defense paradigm for controlling model behavior, potentially applicable to other safety concerns beyond jailbreaks and extensible to other multimodal architectures. It also raises questions about the relationship between interpretability and robustness in complex multimodal systems.
